ScienceDirect: Technical Evaluation of Passive Evaporative Coolers for Post-Harvest Storage

technical_documents.description: An off-grid, zero-electricity post-harvest preservation infrastructure engineered for tropical smallholders. Utilizing a bio-climatic double-brick envelope insulated with a high-surface-area activated charcoal core, this room triggers autonomous heat exchange via capillary drip evaporation, drastically expanding the shelf life of fresh agricultural produce at zero ongoing utility cost.
technical_documents.problem_statement: Post-harvest vegetable spoilage eliminates up to 40% of total smallholder crop yields in humid tropical regions due to the absolute absence of affordable cold-chain infrastructure. With commercial grid-dependent or diesel-powered mechanical cooling units costing upwards of $200, local farmers face continuous revenue loss and reduced market access.
technical_documents.engineering_solution: The architecture deploys a 2m x 2m inner-and-outer clay brick wall structure enclosing a 15cm geometric cavity filled with highly porous activated charcoal. Water is gravity-fed to a regulated micro-drip line from an elevated 200L reservoir, continuously saturating the charcoal matrix. As high-temperature dry ambient air (up to 35°C) passes through the wet, high-surface-area charcoal boundaries, a steady latent heat of vaporization drop is triggered. This thermodynamic cycle drives down internal temperatures by 8–12°C below ambient conditions, stabilizing a cold storage micro-climate of 14–18°C and 85% relative humidity strictly through vapor pressure dynamics.

bill_of_materials.name: High-Surface-Area Granular Activated Charcoal Media (40 kg)
200L High-Density Water Reservoir & Gravity Drip Line Kit
Thermal Thatch Insulated Shading Roof Framework
Analog Precision Volumetric Thermometer & Humidity Sensor

specifications.thermal_reduction: 8–12°C dynamic internal climate drop via passive charcoal evaporation matrix

step_by_step_build: Step 1: Lay a concrete slab base with a 2% drainage slope to one corner.
Step 2: Build inner and outer brick walls with 15cm gap; leave door opening on north side.
Step 3: Fill the wall cavity with activated charcoal, tamping lightly every 30cm.
Step 4: Install drip line along the top of each wall connected to elevated water drum.
Step 5: Set drip rate at 2–3 litres/hour per wall; adjust based on interior thermometer readings to maintain 85% RH.

comments.text: Keeps our tomatoes fresh for 12 days instead of 3. Built for under 55 XLM total.
